abstract:
Test-Time Training (TTT) models context dependencies by adapting part of the
model's weights (referred to as fast weights) during inference. This fast
weight, akin to recurrent states in RNNs, stores temporary memories of past
tokens in the current sequence. Existing TTT methods struggled to show
effectiveness in handling long-context data, due to their inefficiency on
modern GPUs. The TTT layers in many of these approaches operate with extremely
low FLOPs utilization (often <5%) because they deliberately apply small online
minibatch sizes (e.g., updating fast weights every 16 or 64 tokens). Moreover,
a small minibatch implies fine-grained block-wise causal dependencies in the
data, unsuitable for data beyond 1D ordered sequences, like sets or
N-dimensional grids such as images or videos. In contrast, we pursue the
opposite direction by using an extremely large chunk update, ranging from 2K to
1M tokens across tasks of varying modalities, which we refer to as Large Chunk
Test-Time Training (LaCT). It improves hardware utilization by orders of
magnitude, and more importantly, facilitates scaling of nonlinear state size
(up to 40% of model parameters), hence substantially improving state capacity,
all without requiring cumbersome and error-prone kernel implementations. It
also allows easy integration of sophisticated optimizers, e.g. Muon for online
updates. We validate our approach across diverse modalities and tasks,
including novel view synthesis with image set, language models, and
auto-regressive video diffusion. Our approach can scale up to 14B-parameter AR
video diffusion model on sequences up to 56K tokens. In our longest sequence
experiment, we perform novel view synthesis with 1 million context length. We
hope this work will inspire and accelerate new research in the field of
long-context modeling and test-time training. Website:
this https URL

keyInsights: Large chunk sizes enable high GPU utilization and simple PyTorch implementation while maintaining the core benefits of test-time adaptation
Muon optimizer with orthogonalized matrix gradients and L2 normalization significantly outperforms vanilla gradient descent and momentum for fast-weight updates
Hybrid architecture combining quadratic local attention with linear global TTT leverages strengths of both paradigms
Decoupled update and apply operations with flexible masking naturally extend TTT to diverse N-dimensional data structures
originalProblem: Existing test-time training methods suffer from extremely low GPU utilization (below 5%) due to small online mini-batch sizes (16-64 tokens), requiring cumbersome custom kernel implementations
Previous TTT approaches are limited to small fast-weight networks due to computational bottlenecks, constraining memory capacity for long-context modeling
Fine-grained causal dependencies make existing TTT methods unsuitable for N-dimensional data like images or videos beyond 1D sequences
Implementation complexity hinders rapid research exploration and democratization of TTT research
results: Novel view synthesis: 1.4s prefill time vs 16.1s for full attention on 196K tokens, achieving 39.1 PSNR vs 33.7 for Perceiver baselines at 512x512 resolution
Language modeling: consistently lower per-position loss and superior retrieval accuracy compared to GLA and DeltaNet on sequences up to 32K tokens
Video diffusion: 14B-parameter model handling 56,000 visual tokens with comparable validation loss to full attention while outperforming Mamba and sliding window baselines
GPU utilization reaches 70% on NVIDIA A100s without custom kernels, enabling practical deployment and broader research accessibility
solution: Shift from small mini-batch updates to extremely large chunk updates (2K-1M tokens) to maximize parallelism and hardware utilization
Scale nonlinear fast-weight networks (SwiGLU-MLP) up to 40% of total model parameters for enhanced state capacity
Integrate window attention with large-chunk TTT to handle local dependencies while TTT manages non-local context
Support diverse attention masks (full, block-wise causal, strided) to adapt to different data modalities and dependency structures
paper_summary.summary: MIT and Adobe Research researchers develop LaCT (Large Chunk Test-Time Training), a framework that transforms test-time training from inefficient small mini-batch updates to extremely large chunk updates (2K to 1M tokens), achieving 70% GPU utilization while scaling nonlinear fast-weight networks up to 40% of model parameter size, demonstrating superior performance across three diverse tasks: novel view synthesis with 1M+ context length (39.1 PSNR vs 33.7 for Perceiver), long-context language modeling outperforming GLA and DeltaNet on retrieval accuracy, and 14B-parameter autoregressive video diffusion handling 56,000 visual tokens through pure PyTorch implementation that eliminates the need for custom kernels while integrating window attention for local dependencies.

title: Test-Time Training Done Right

abstract: Modern Recurrent Neural Networks have become a competitive architecture for 3D reconstruction due to their linear-time complexity. However, their performance degrades significantly when applied beyond the training context length, revealing limited length generalization. In this work, we revisit the 3D reconstruction foundation models from a Test-Time Training perspective, framing their designs as an online learning problem. Building on this perspective, we leverage the alignment confidence between the memory state and incoming observations to derive a closed-form learning rate for memory updates, to balance between retaining historical information and adapting to new observations. This training-free intervention, termed TTT3R, substantially improves length generalization, achieving a $2\times$ improvement in global pose estimation over baselines, while operating at 20 FPS with just 6 GB of GPU memory to process thousands of images. Code is available in this https URL

keyInsights: The standard `softmax` operation in existing RNN state updates implicitly sets a fixed learning rate, forcing constant prioritization of new information and leading to catastrophic forgetting of historical context.

An adaptive, confidence-guided learning rate for the memory state update can effectively balance the retention of historical information with the integration of new observations, thereby improving length generalization.
Re-framing the RNN state update as a Test-Time Training process provides a principled, training-free, and plug-and-play mechanism to enhance existing pre-trained recurrent 3D reconstruction models.
originalProblem: Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) based 3D reconstruction models suffer from the "forgetting problem" and poor length generalization, leading to performance degradation on long input sequences.
Existing online methods often struggle to maintain accuracy and memory efficiency over thousands of frames, either succumbing to out-of-memory errors or significant performance drops.
Offline Transformer-based methods, while accurate, incur quadratic computational and memory costs, rendering them impractical for real-time, long-sequence online processing.
results: Achieved a 2x improvement in Absolute Translation Error (ATE) for camera pose estimation on long sequences (e.g., 1000 views on ScanNet and TUM-D) compared to the CUT3R baseline.

Consistently improved video depth estimation and 3D reconstruction accuracy, mitigating artifacts and achieving geometric quality comparable to computationally heavier offline methods on long sequences.

Operates at 20 Frames Per Second (FPS) with a constant 6 GB GPU memory footprint, enabling real-time, long-sequence processing without additional computational overhead or requiring retraining of the base model.
solution: Reinterprets the RNN state update mechanism in recurrent 3D reconstruction models (specifically CUT3R) as a Test-Time Training (TTT) process with dynamically updated "fast weights."

Proposes a confidence-guided state update rule that introduces an adaptive, per-token learning rate for the memory state, which is derived from the alignment confidence between the memory and incoming observations.
Includes an optional periodic state reset mechanism to further prevent state overfitting and retain efficiency for extremely long sequences beyond 1000 frames.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University, Westlake University, University of Tübingen, and Tübingen AI Center developed TTT3R, a training-free approach to enhance recurrent neural network-based 3D reconstruction models. It introduces a confidence-guided state update rule that significantly improves length generalization and mitigates forgetting, reducing Absolute Translation Error by 2x on long sequences while maintaining real-time efficiency and constant memory usage.

title: TTT3R: 3D Reconstruction as Test-Time Training

abstract: Perceiving and reconstructing 3D geometry from videos is a fundamental yet challenging computer vision task. To facilitate interactive and low-latency applications, we propose a streaming visual geometry transformer that shares a similar philosophy with autoregressive large language models. We explore a simple and efficient design and employ a causal transformer architecture to process the input sequence in an online manner. We use temporal causal attention and cache the historical keys and values as implicit memory to enable efficient streaming long-term 3D reconstruction. This design can handle low-latency 3D reconstruction by incrementally integrating historical information while maintaining high-quality spatial consistency. For efficient training, we propose to distill knowledge from the dense bidirectional visual geometry grounded transformer (VGGT) to our causal model. For inference, our model supports the migration of optimized efficient attention operators (e.g., FlashAttention) from large language models. Extensive experiments on various 3D geometry perception benchmarks demonstrate that our model enhances inference speed in online scenarios while maintaining competitive performance, thereby facilitating scalable and interactive 3D vision systems. Code is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Causal transformer architectures with cached memory effectively convert high-accuracy offline 3D reconstruction models into efficient, low-latency streaming systems.
Knowledge distillation from a powerful teacher model significantly improves the performance and reduces the training cost of a causal streaming student model.
Combining causal attention with optimized attention operators (FlashAttention-2) and strategic memory caching enables scalable and robust real-time 3D reconstruction for long video sequences.
originalProblem: Traditional 3D reconstruction methods are computationally intensive and designed for offline processing, struggling with real-time, incremental updates or dynamic environments.

State-of-the-art learning-based global interaction models achieve high accuracy but require reprocessing the entire sequence with each new frame, leading to quadratic computational costs and high latency.
Existing streaming methods often accumulate errors over long sequences or incur heavy computational demands, struggling to balance efficiency with consistent, high-quality reconstruction.
results: StreamVGGT achieved accuracy comparable to the state-of-the-art offline model VGGT on ETH3D and surpassed other streaming methods on 7-Scenes and NRGBD datasets for 3D reconstruction.

The model demonstrated significantly reduced inference latency (up to 2.4x faster per frame) and lower memory consumption (up to 2.2x less) compared to its teacher VGGT, crucial for real-time operation.
Robust performance was shown in camera pose estimation, single-frame and video depth estimation, and 4D reconstruction in dynamic scenarios, maintaining temporal consistency over extended sequences.
solution: StreamVGGT was developed as a causal transformer architecture that processes video frames incrementally using temporal causal attention.
A cached memory token mechanism was integrated during inference to efficiently reuse historical keys and values, enabling consistent contextual understanding without reprocessing past frames.
A knowledge distillation strategy was employed, training StreamVGGT by supervising it with dense pseudo-labels from a high-performing offline teacher model (VGGT) to reduce training resources.
paper_summary.summary: A causal transformer model, StreamVGGT, was developed at Tsinghua University for efficient, low-latency 3D geometry reconstruction from streaming video inputs. It leverages a cached memory mechanism and knowledge distillation to provide incremental scene updates with accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art offline methods, achieving up to 2.4x faster inference and 2.2x less memory usage on shorter sequences than its teacher model.

title: Streaming 4D Visual Geometry Transformer

abstract:
Emerging world models autoregressively generate video frames in response to
actions, such as camera movements and text prompts, among other control
signals. Due to limited temporal context window sizes, these models often
struggle to maintain scene consistency during revisits, leading to severe
forgetting of previously generated environments. Inspired by the mechanisms of
human memory, we introduce a novel framework to enhancing long-term consistency
of video world models through a geometry-grounded long-term spatial memory. Our
framework includes mechanisms to store and retrieve information from the
long-term spatial memory and we curate custom datasets to train and evaluate
world models with explicitly stored 3D memory mechanisms. Our evaluations show
improved quality, consistency, and context length compared to relevant
baselines, paving the way towards long-term consistent world generation.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Human-inspired memory mechanisms can effectively address temporal consistency challenges in generative models through explicit geometric representation
Static scene geometry can be disentangled from dynamic content using TSDF fusion, which naturally suppresses inconsistent dynamic elements
Combining geometry-grounded spatial memory with sparse visual episodic memory provides both structural consistency and fine-grained detail preservation
Point cloud rendering as conditioning signal enables precise camera control while maintaining static background coherence across arbitrary time horizons
originalProblem: Current video world models suffer from severe "forgetting" when revisiting previously generated scenes due to limited temporal context windows in autoregressive generation
Existing approaches rely primarily on image-based representations of the past, lacking persistent 3D understanding of world geometry
Models struggle to maintain long-term consistency between static background elements and dynamic foreground content over extended video sequences
Computational constraints from quadratic attention complexity prevent processing of numerous past frames simultaneously
results: Achieved 19.10 PSNR vs 11.71-12.16 for baselines on view recall consistency tasks, with corresponding improvements in SSIM (0.6471 vs 0.4380-0.4512) and LPIPS (0.3069 vs 0.5874-0.5996)
Superior VBench performance across aesthetic quality (0.5835), temporal flickering (0.7580), motion smoothness (0.9886), and subject consistency (0.9359)
Human evaluation rankings of 3.6260 for camera accuracy, 3.3846 for static consistency, and 3.4011 for dynamic plausibility, significantly outperforming all baseline methods
Ablation studies confirm each memory component contributes essential functionality, with full model achieving best results across all evaluated metrics
solution: Implement three-tier memory system: working memory (recent frames), spatial memory (3D point clouds), and episodic memory (sparse keyframes)
Use TSDF fusion to extract and maintain persistent static scene geometry, automatically filtering out dynamic elements through depth consistency
Integrate memory mechanisms into diffusion transformer via condition latents from point cloud rendering and historical cross-attention modules
Create custom training dataset with explicit 3D reconstructions and paired static/dynamic annotations using Mega-SaM and action labeling
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Stanford University, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, NTU, and CUHK develop a memory-augmented video world model that overcomes the "forgetting problem" in long-term video generation by implementing three distinct memory mechanisms inspired by human cognition: working memory (recent context frames), geometry-grounded spatial memory (persistent 3D point clouds of static scenes updated via TSDF fusion), and sparse episodic memory (keyframes from previously explored regions), achieving substantial improvements in view recall consistency with 19.10 vs 11.71-12.16 PSNR when revisiting camera poses and demonstrating superior performance across VBench metrics for aesthetic quality (0.5835), temporal flickering (0.7580), and motion smoothness (0.9886) compared to baselines like TrajectoryCrafter and DaS, with the framework built upon CogVideoX-5B-I2V and trained on a custom 90K-sample dataset where static point clouds are extracted using CUT3R reconstruction and TSDF filtering to suppress dynamic elements, enabling infinite-length consistent world generation through explicit 3D geometry understanding rather than relying solely on image-based temporal contexts.

title: Video World Models with Long-term Spatial Memory

abstract: Humans perceive and understand real-world spaces through a stream of visual observations. Therefore, the ability to streamingly maintain and update spatial evidence from potentially unbounded video streams is essential for spatial intelligence. The core challenge is not simply longer context windows but how spatial information is selected, organized, and retained over time. In this paper, we propose Spatial-TTT towards streaming visual-based spatial intelligence with test-time training (TTT), which adapts a subset of parameters (fast weights) to capture and organize spatial evidence over long-horizon scene videos. Specifically, we design a hybrid architecture and adopt large-chunk updates parallel with sliding-window attention for efficient spatial video processing. To further promote spatial awareness, we introduce a spatial-predictive mechanism applied to TTT layers with 3D spatiotemporal convolution, which encourages the model to capture geometric correspondence and temporal continuity across frames. Beyond architecture design, we construct a dataset with dense 3D spatial descriptions, which guides the model to update its fast weights to memorize and organize global 3D spatial signals in a structured manner. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Spatial-TTT improves long-horizon spatial understanding and achieves state-of-the-art performance on video spatial benchmarks. Project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Test-time training, when combined with a hybrid architecture and spatial-predictive mechanisms, can effectively build a compact and adaptive spatial memory for MLLMs to process and reason over long streaming videos.

Injecting spatiotemporal inductive biases via 3D convolutions into TTT layers is crucial for enhancing the model's ability to capture geometric correspondence and temporal continuity, leading to improved metric-level 3D spatial reasoning.

Dense, scene-level spatial descriptions provide a significantly richer supervisory signal than sparse spatial QA data, enabling the model to learn more effective fast-weight update dynamics for accumulating and organizing global 3D spatial evidence persistently.
originalProblem: Current MLLMs struggle with precise 3D geometric understanding and spatial reasoning, often degrading on tasks requiring it due to their reliance on 2D semantic-level training data.
Processing long-horizon, streaming visual observations (videos) with MLLMs incurs prohibitive computational and memory costs from the quadratic complexity of standard self-attention mechanisms.

Existing methods for enhancing spatial awareness in MLLMs are limited to single images or short video clips, failing to scale to continuous, unbounded visual streams encountered in real-world applications.
results: Spatial-TTT-2B achieved an overall average score of 64.4 on VSI-Bench, outperforming all proprietary and open-source baselines, and attained 76.2% accuracy on MindCube-Tiny, surpassing Gemini-3-pro by 12.3 percentage points.

The model demonstrated robust performance on streaming spatial sensing tasks (VSI-SUPER-Recall and VSI-SUPER-Count) over long-horizon videos (up to 120 minutes), significantly outperforming baselines in continuous object counting.

Ablation studies confirmed that the spatial-predictive mechanism, dense scene-description supervision, and hybrid TTT architecture are critical components, each contributing to improved performance, and Spatial-TTT exhibited approximately linear scaling of resource usage.
solution: Spatial-TTT introduces a framework that integrates Test-Time Training (TTT) into MLLMs, allowing a subset of model parameters to continually adapt during inference for streaming visual-based spatial intelligence.

A hybrid TTT architecture combines TTT layers with standard self-attention layers, uses large chunk updates, and incorporates parallel sliding window attention for efficient, approximately linear-complexity processing of long video streams.

A spatial-predictive mechanism uses depth-wise 3D spatiotemporal convolutions to inject inductive biases, encouraging fast weights to capture geometric correspondence and temporal continuity for coherent memory updates; the Muon update rule stabilizes these updates.
paper_summary.summary: Spatial-TTT equips Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with the ability to process and reason about 3D spaces from continuous video streams using a test-time training framework. It integrates a hybrid architecture and spatial-predictive mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art performance, including a 12.3 percentage point improvement on MindCube-Tiny and robust object counting over 120-minute videos.

title: Spatial-TTT: Streaming Visual-based Spatial Intelligence with Test-Time Training

abstract: We present a scalable 3D reconstruction model that addresses a critical limitation in offline feed-forward methods: their computational and memory requirements grow quadratically w.r.t. the number of input images. Our approach is built on the key insight that this bottleneck stems from the varying-length Key-Value (KV) space representation of scene geometry, which we distill into a fixed-size Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) via test-time training. VGG-T$^3$ (Visual Geometry Grounded Test Time Training) scales linearly w.r.t. the number of input views, similar to online models, and reconstructs a $1k$ image collection in just $54$ seconds, achieving a $11.6\times$ speed-up over baselines that rely on softmax attention. Since our method retains global scene aggregation capability, our point map reconstruction error outperforming other linear-time methods by large margins. Finally, we demonstrate visual localization capabilities of our model by querying the scene representation with unseen images.

keyInsights: Distilling a Transformer's scene representation into a fixed-size MLP through test-time training can effectively reduce computational complexity from quadratic to linear, enabling large-scale processing.
Pre-training with softmax attention and then linearizing the model's QKV projections is crucial for achieving high accuracy with TTT, outperforming training from scratch.
The novel MLP-based scene representation allows for a unified approach to both 3D scene reconstruction (mapping) and efficient feed-forward visual localization of new images.
originalProblem: Existing Transformer-based 3D reconstruction models suffer from quadratic computational and memory complexity (O(n^2)) with respect to the number of input images, making them impractical for large-scale 'in-the-wild' datasets.
Current learning-based approaches struggle to balance the high accuracy of global scene understanding with the efficiency required for processing thousands of unordered images offline.
results: VGG-T3 achieves a significant 11.6x speed-up over VGGT, reconstructing 1,000 images in 54-58 seconds, and demonstrates linear speed-ups with multi-GPU distributed inference.

The model substantially outperforms other linear-time methods (e.g., TTT3R) in pointmap estimation, reducing Chamfer Distance by 2-2.5x on several benchmarks while remaining competitive with quadratic-time baselines.

VGG-T3 successfully performs feed-forward visual localization, outperforming TTT3R on both 7scenes and Wayspots datasets, demonstrating its ability to localize unseen images effectively within a reconstructed scene.
solution: The VGG-T3 method replaces the global self-attention layers in a pre-trained VGGT (Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer) with a novel Test-Time Training (TTT) mechanism.

During TTT, the variable-length Key-Value (KV) space, implicitly representing scene geometry, is distilled into a fixed-size Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) that learns the K->V mapping in a self-supervised manner.
Enhancements like an L2-normalization-based linearization of pre-trained weights and a 2D convolution (ShortConv2D) for non-linear spatial mixing improve performance and robustness.
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA researchers present VGG-T3, an offline feed-forward 3D reconstruction framework that achieves linear computational and memory complexity (O(n)) by distilling a Transformer's variable-length scene representation into a fixed-size Multi-Layer Perceptron via test-time training. The method processes 1,000 images in under a minute, demonstrating an 11.6x speed-up over its quadratic baseline, and also enables effective visual localization.

title: VGG-T$^3$: Offline Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction at Scale

abstract: Can we scale 4D pretraining to learn general space-time representations that reconstruct an object from a few views at some times to any view at any time? We provide an affirmative answer with 4D-LRM, the first large-scale 4D reconstruction model that takes input from unconstrained views and timestamps and renders arbitrary novel view-time combinations. Unlike prior 4D approaches, e.g., optimization-based, geometry-based, or generative, that struggle with efficiency, generalization, or faithfulness, 4D-LRM learns a unified space-time representation and directly predicts per-pixel 4D Gaussian primitives from posed image tokens across time, enabling fast, high-quality rendering at, in principle, infinite frame rate. Our results demonstrate that scaling spatiotemporal pretraining enables accurate and efficient 4D reconstruction. We show that 4D-LRM generalizes to novel objects, interpolates across time, and handles diverse camera setups. It reconstructs 24-frame sequences in one forward pass with less than 1.5 seconds on a single A100 GPU.

keyInsights: Large-scale spatiotemporal pretraining with a Transformer architecture can learn a generalizable space-time representation for 4D reconstruction from sparse inputs.
A unified 4D Gaussian Splatting representation is effective for jointly modeling spatial structure and temporal dynamics, resolving motion ambiguities by sharing information across views and time.
Knowledge transfer from pre-trained 3D LRMs significantly boosts performance and accelerates convergence for dynamic 4D reconstruction tasks.
originalProblem: Reconstructing complete 4D (space-time) representations of dynamic objects from limited visual inputs is computationally expensive and often lacks generalization.
Existing 4D reconstruction methods struggle with efficiency, requiring dense inputs or per-scene optimization, or prioritize perceptual quality over geometric faithfulness when inputs are sparse.
Current Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs) are designed only for static 3D content, limiting their application to dynamic scenes.
results: 4D-LRM consistently achieves high reconstruction quality (e.g., 32.177 PSNR on Consistent4D at 256 resolution) and efficiency, reconstructing a 24-frame dynamic object in less than 1.5 seconds on a single A100 GPU.
It demonstrates robustness and generalization to challenging input conditions, outperforming prior methods in quality, especially with sparse views and effectively interpolating missing frames.
When fine-tuned for 4D generation, 4D-LRM produces more geometrically accurate dynamic objects compared to existing generative models, enhancing the faithfulness and speed of 4D asset creation.
solution: The paper introduces 4D-LRM, a Transformer-based model that directly regresses 4D Gaussian primitives to represent dynamic objects.
It utilizes 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) as a unified space-time representation, where each Gaussian includes both spatial and temporal centers and anisotropic spatiotemporal covariance.
The model takes temporally posed image tokens (RGB, Plücker ray coordinates, and timestamp) as input, processes them through a Transformer, and decodes them into pixel-aligned 4D Gaussian primitives.
paper_summary.summary: 4D-LRM introduces a Transformer-based Large Reconstruction Model that leverages 4D Gaussian Splatting to reconstruct dynamic objects from sparse, unconstrained input views and timestamps into full space-time representations. The model reconstructs a 24-frame dynamic object in under 1.5 seconds on an A100 GPU, achieving high fidelity and generalizing to novel view-time combinations.

title: 4D-LRM: Large Space-Time Reconstruction Model From and To Any View at Any Time

abstract: Feedforward geometric foundation models achieve strong short-window reconstruction, yet scaling them to minutes-long videos is bottlenecked by quadratic attention complexity or limited effective memory in recurrent designs. We present LoGeR (Long-context Geometric Reconstruction), a novel architecture that scales dense 3D reconstruction to extremely long sequences without post-optimization. LoGeR processes video streams in chunks, leveraging strong bidirectional priors for high-fidelity intra-chunk reasoning. To manage the critical challenge of coherence across chunk boundaries, we propose a learning-based hybrid memory module. This dual-component system combines a parametric Test-Time Training (TTT) memory to anchor the global coordinate frame and prevent scale drift, alongside a non-parametric Sliding Window Attention (SWA) mechanism to preserve uncompressed context for high-precision adjacent alignment. Remarkably, this memory architecture enables LoGeR to be trained on sequences of 128 frames, and generalize up to thousands of frames during inference. Evaluated across standard benchmarks and a newly repurposed VBR dataset with sequences of up to 19k frames, LoGeR substantially outperforms prior state-of-the-art feedforward methods--reducing ATE on KITTI by over 74%--and achieves robust, globally consistent reconstruction over unprecedented horizons.

keyInsights: A dual-component hybrid memory effectively addresses the multi-scale coherence problem in dense 3D reconstruction by balancing global consistency and local detail across chunk boundaries.
Parametric TTT layers serve as an associative, evolving memory, crucial for anchoring the global coordinate frame and preventing scale drift over thousands of frames.
Sparse SWA layers act as an efficient information highway, providing high-precision feature propagation for seamless alignment between adjacent video chunks.
originalProblem: Feedforward geometric models face a 'context wall' due to the quadratic complexity of global attention, limiting them to short video segments (dozens to a hundred frames).
A 'data wall' exists as most training data consists of short sequences, making models struggle to generalize to long-horizon, kilometer-scale environments.
Achieving robust, globally consistent dense 3D reconstruction over thousands of frames in a purely feedforward manner, without computationally intensive post-optimization, remains a challenge.
results: LoGeR* achieves an average Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) of 18.65m on the KITTI benchmark, representing a 74% reduction compared to prior feedforward methods like TTT3R, and outperforms some optimization-based SLAM systems.
On the VBR dataset, LoGeR* yields an average ATE of 5.27m for sequences up to 20,000 frames, a 30.8% relative improvement over previous feedforward approaches.

The framework also achieves strong performance on short-context benchmarks (e.g., 7-Scenes, ScanNetV2, TUM-Dynamics), showing significant relative gains in Chamfer Distance and ATE for both 3D reconstruction and camera pose estimation.
solution: The system employs chunk-wise processing for long video sequences, using minimal overlap to bound computational cost while leveraging strong local priors from existing short-context models.

It introduces a novel hybrid memory module combining Parametric Test-Time Training (TTT) layers for lossy, long-term global context compression and Non-Parametric Sliding Window Attention (SWA) for lossless, short-term high-fidelity local alignment.

A progressive curriculum training strategy is utilized on a diverse mixture of large-scale scene datasets to enable generalization from limited short-context training data to extremely long sequences at inference time.
paper_summary.summary: A framework developed by Google DeepMind and UC Berkeley allows feedforward geometric models to achieve robust, globally consistent dense 3D reconstruction from video sequences thousands of frames long. It reduces Absolute Trajectory Error by over 74% on the KITTI benchmark, surpassing previous feedforward methods and some optimization-based SLAM systems.

title: LoGeR: Long-Context Geometric Reconstruction with Hybrid Memory

abstract: We propose tttLRM, a novel large 3D reconstruction model that leverages a Test-Time Training (TTT) layer to enable long-context, autoregressive 3D reconstruction with linear computational complexity, further scaling the model's capability. Our framework efficiently compresses multiple image observations into the fast weights of the TTT layer, forming an implicit 3D representation in the latent space that can be decoded into various explicit formats, such as Gaussian Splats (GS) for downstream applications. The online learning variant of our model supports progressive 3D reconstruction and refinement from streaming observations. We demonstrate that pretraining on novel view synthesis tasks effectively transfers to explicit 3D modeling, resulting in improved reconstruction quality and faster convergence. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves superior performance in feedforward 3D Gaussian reconstruction compared to state-of-the-art approaches on both objects and scenes.

keyInsights: Test-Time Training with Large Chunk (LaCT) blocks provides an effective mechanism for handling long input sequences with linear complexity, overcoming the quadratic bottlenecks of traditional attention.
Pretraining on novel view synthesis tasks, such as with TTT-LVSM, establishes strong priors that significantly accelerate convergence and enhance the quality of explicit 3D reconstruction models.
An autoregressive strategy that performs full reconstruction at each step is more effective than a "Predict & Merge" approach, preventing error accumulation in sequential processing.
originalProblem: Existing Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs) are limited to a small number of input views (e.g., 4-32) due to the quadratic computational complexity of attention mechanisms, hindering scalability for large scenes.
Methods for 3D reconstruction from streaming visual input generally lack efficient long-context processing and memory mechanisms for progressive updates.

A trade-off often exists between the high rendering quality of implicit neural representations (like NeRFs) and the real-time efficiency of explicit representations (like 3D Gaussian Splatting), lacking a unified solution.
results: tttLRM surpasses attention-based baselines like GS-LRM by achieving up to a 1 dB PSNR improvement at 512x512 resolution while being twice as fast, and scales effectively to 1024x1024 resolutions without out-of-memory issues.

It outperforms Long-LRM by approximately 1 dB PSNR on scene-level reconstruction tasks (e.g., DL3DV-140) and is hundreds of times faster than optimization-based methods like 3DGS, while maintaining competitive quality for up to 64 views.

The model effectively supports autoregressive reconstruction, progressively improving rendering quality and scene coverage from streaming data, demonstrating robust generalization across datasets and input view counts.
solution: tttLRM leverages Test-Time Training (TTT) and Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT) blocks to update "fast weights" during inference, enabling linear computational complexity with respect to input sequence length.

The architecture tokenizes input images and their ray embeddings, feeding them into LaCT blocks to encode scene information into fast weights, which are then queried by virtual tokens to decode explicit 3D representations like Gaussian Splats.
It supports autoregressive reconstruction by incrementally updating fast weights with new input views, allowing for progressive refinement of the 3D representation from streaming data.
paper_summary.summary: tttLRM presents a 3D reconstruction model that performs high-resolution, long-context, and autoregressive 3D reconstruction from streaming visual input using Test-Time Training. This approach achieves up to 1 dB PSNR improvement over prior state-of-the-art feedforward methods and maintains competitive performance with optimization-based approaches while being hundreds of times faster.

title: tttLRM: Test-Time Training for Long Context and Autoregressive 3D Reconstruction

abstract: LongSplat addresses critical challenges in novel view synthesis (NVS) from casually captured long videos characterized by irregular camera motion, unknown camera poses, and expansive scenes. Current methods often suffer from pose drift, inaccurate geometry initialization, and severe memory limitations. To address these issues, we introduce LongSplat, a robust unposed 3D Gaussian Splatting framework featuring: (1) Incremental Joint Optimization that concurrently optimizes camera poses and 3D Gaussians to avoid local minima and ensure global consistency; (2) a robust Pose Estimation Module leveraging learned 3D priors; and (3) an efficient Octree Anchor Formation mechanism that converts dense point clouds into anchors based on spatial density. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate that LongSplat achieves state-of-the-art results, substantially improving rendering quality, pose accuracy, and computational efficiency compared to prior approaches. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Tightly coupling incremental pose estimation with 3D Gaussian Splatting optimization within a visibility-adapted local window effectively prevents accumulated drift and maintains global scene coherence over long sequences.
Leveraging monocular depth and keypoint priors can robustly initialize and constrain camera poses and scene geometry, overcoming the limitations of traditional SfM for casual footage.

An adaptive octree structure provides a memory-efficient and spatially flexible way to manage 3D Gaussians in continuously expanding, large-scale environments, optimizing for both density and coverage.
originalProblem: Traditional Structure-from-Motion (SfM) methods frequently fail or produce inaccurate camera poses for casually captured long videos with irregular motion or textureless regions.
Existing unposed Novel View Synthesis (NVS) techniques struggle with accumulated pose drift, fragmentation, and memory constraints when applied to long, unconstrained video sequences.
Scaling 3D reconstruction and NVS to large-scale environments captured in long videos presents significant memory and computational challenges for both implicit and explicit scene representations.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art novel view synthesis quality, with average PSNRs of 32.83 dB on Tanks and Temples and 27.88 dB on the Free dataset, outperforming existing methods.
Demonstrates superior camera pose accuracy, exhibiting significantly lower pose errors (e.g., ATE of 0.004 and RPE translation of 0.028 on the Free dataset) compared to baselines.
Offers high computational efficiency, training in approximately 1 hour on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 (281.71 FPS) while maintaining a compact model size of 101 MB.
solution: An incremental joint optimization framework processes video frames sequentially, simultaneously refining camera poses and 3D Gaussian parameters to ensure global consistency.
A robust pose estimation module uses PnP initialization with monocular depth priors (MASt3R), photometric refinement, and depth scale correction for accurate unposed camera tracking.
An efficient octree anchor formation mechanism employs density-guided adaptive voxelization to structure 3D Gaussians, enabling memory-efficient and scalable representation of large scenes.
paper_summary.summary: LongSplat, developed by researchers at National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University and NVIDIA Research, introduces a robust 3D Gaussian Splatting framework that reconstructs detailed scenes and accurate camera poses from unposed, casually captured long videos. It achieves superior rendering quality and pose accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency for expansive environments.

title: LongSplat: Robust Unposed 3D Gaussian Splatting for Casual Long Videos

abstract: This paper aims to address the challenge of reconstructing long volumetric videos from multi-view RGB videos. Recent dynamic view synthesis methods leverage powerful 4D representations, like feature grids or point cloud sequences, to achieve high-quality rendering results. However, they are typically limited to short (1~2s) video clips and often suffer from large memory footprints when dealing with longer videos. To solve this issue, we propose a novel 4D representation, named Temporal Gaussian Hierarchy, to compactly model long volumetric videos. Our key observation is that there are generally various degrees of temporal redundancy in dynamic scenes, which consist of areas changing at different speeds. Motivated by this, our approach builds a multi-level hierarchy of 4D Gaussian primitives, where each level separately describes scene regions with different degrees of content change, and adaptively shares Gaussian primitives to represent unchanged scene content over different temporal segments, thus effectively reducing the number of Gaussian primitives. In addition, the tree-like structure of the Gaussian hierarchy allows us to efficiently represent the scene at a particular moment with a subset of Gaussian primitives, leading to nearly constant GPU memory usage during the training or rendering regardless of the video length. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method over alternative methods in terms of training cost, rendering speed, and storage usage. To our knowledge, this work is the first approach capable of efficiently handling minutes of volumetric video data while maintaining state-of-the-art rendering quality. Our project page is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Hierarchical temporal segmentation allows for maintaining nearly constant GPU memory usage and iteration speed during training and rendering, irrespective of the video's total length.
Adaptive placement of Gaussians within the hierarchy based on their temporal influence range optimizes resource allocation and prevents redundant processing across segments.
A hybrid appearance model effectively balances model size reduction with the preservation of view-dependent visual quality by selectively compressing less critical appearance parameters.
originalProblem: Existing state-of-the-art 4D dynamic scene representations like 4D Gaussian Splatting incur prohibitive memory and computational costs when extended to long volumetric videos (minutes-long).
Current methods fail to exploit the inherent temporal redundancy in dynamic scenes, treating all parts uniformly despite varying motion speeds.
Achieving real-time rendering for extended dynamic content remains challenging due to the scalability limitations of existing high-fidelity reconstruction techniques.
results: The method processes 18,000-frame videos (10+ minutes) with only 17.2 GB VRAM and 2.2 GB storage, demonstrating a 30x VRAM and 26x storage reduction compared to prior methods like 4K4D.
Achieves state-of-the-art visual quality across diverse public datasets (e.g., Neural3DV, ENeRF-Outdoor) and on a new 2-10 minute 4K SelfCap dataset.
Delivers real-time rendering performance of up to 450 FPS at 1080p resolution, representing a 5x speedup over software rasterization and making it suitable for interactive applications.
solution: A Temporal Gaussian Hierarchy (TGH) organizes 4D Gaussian primitives into a multi-level structure with varying temporal segment lengths, allowing efficient loading of only relevant segments into GPU memory.
A Compact Appearance Model utilizes sparse spherical harmonics and gradient-based thresholding to differentiate and compress appearance information for diffuse Gaussians, reducing overall model size.
A specialized real-time rendering pipeline employs CUDA-based sorting of Gaussians combined with hardware-accelerated GPU rasterization for high frame rates during inference.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University, Stanford University, and the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology introduce Temporal Gaussian Hierarchy (TGH), a novel 4D representation for efficiently reconstructing and rendering long volumetric videos. This method handles 10+ minute videos with 30x less VRAM and 26x less storage than prior state-of-the-art techniques, while maintaining state-of-the-art visual quality and achieving real-time rendering at up to 450 FPS.

title: Representing Long Volumetric Video with Temporal Gaussian Hierarchy

abstract: In dynamic 3D environments, accurately updating scene representations over time is crucial for applications in robotics, mixed reality, and embodied AI. As scenes evolve, efficient methods to incorporate changes are needed to maintain up-to-date, high-quality reconstructions without the computational overhead of re-optimizing the entire scene. This paper introduces CL-Splats, which incrementally updates Gaussian splatting-based 3D representations from sparse scene captures. CL-Splats integrates a robust change-detection module that segments updated and static components within the scene, enabling focused, local optimization that avoids unnecessary re-computation. Moreover, CL-Splats supports storing and recovering previous scene states, facilitating temporal segmentation and new scene-analysis applications. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that CL-Splats achieves efficient updates with improved reconstruction quality over the state-of-the-art. This establishes a robust foundation for future real-time adaptation in 3D scene reconstruction tasks.

keyInsights: Explicit 3D Gaussian Splatting representations are more efficient for continual learning of dynamic scenes than implicit neural representations, enabling faster updates and real-time rendering.
Precisely identifying and restricting optimization to 3D changed regions is crucial for computational efficiency and maintaining reconstruction quality in static areas.
Freezing static components of the 3D scene during updates is effective in preventing catastrophic forgetting and ensuring consistent scene integrity over time.
originalProblem: Existing 3D reconstruction methods incur high computational overhead when updating dynamic scenes, often requiring full re-optimization.
Prior continual learning approaches for implicit neural representations (like NeRFs) suffer from catastrophic forgetting and are severely limited by slow training and rendering times.
Achieving efficient, high-fidelity 3D scene reconstruction that dynamically adapts to real-world changes is a significant challenge for applications like robotics and mixed reality.
results: CL-Splats achieves an average PSNR of 40.125 on synthetic datasets, demonstrating a substantial improvement over CL-NeRF (30.063 PSNR), while rendering at real-time speeds (e.g., 223 FPS).
It completes high-quality updates in 40 seconds (5K iterations), which is 75 times faster than CL-NeRF's 50 minutes (25K iterations) for less detailed results.

The method enables efficient history recovery by storing only localized changes (36MB/step compared to 1173MB/step for naive storage) and supports concurrent, non-overlapping scene updates without error accumulation.
solution: A three-stage framework detects 2D changes using DINOv2 features and lifts these to 3D via a majority voting mechanism on existing Gaussians, with targeted sampling for new objects.
Local optimization is applied exclusively to the detected 3D changed regions using a modified 3DGS CUDA kernel that dynamically masks rendering and gradient computations.
Static background Gaussians are explicitly 'frozen' by masking their gradients during optimization, preventing catastrophic forgetting and preserving unchanged scene parts.
paper_summary.summary: CL-Splats introduces a framework for continually updating 3D Gaussian Splatting representations in dynamic environments using local optimization. The method achieves high reconstruction quality and real-time performance, outperforming previous NeRF-based continual learning approaches with gains of over 10 PSNR and orders of magnitude faster optimization.

title: CL-Splats: Continual Learning of Gaussian Splatting with Local Optimization

abstract: Recent advancements in multi-view scene reconstruction have been significant, yet existing methods face limitations when processing streams of input images. These methods either rely on time-consuming offline optimization or are restricted to shorter sequences, hindering their applicability in real-time scenarios. In this work, we propose LONG3R (LOng sequence streaming 3D Reconstruction), a novel model designed for streaming multi-view 3D scene reconstruction over longer sequences. Our model achieves real-time processing by operating recurrently, maintaining and updating memory with each new observation. We first employ a memory gating mechanism to filter relevant memory, which, together with a new observation, is fed into a dual-source refined decoder for coarse-to-fine interaction. To effectively capture long-sequence memory, we propose a 3D spatio-temporal memory that dynamically prunes redundant spatial information while adaptively adjusting resolution along the scene. To enhance our model's performance on long sequences while maintaining training efficiency, we employ a two-stage curriculum training strategy, each stage targeting specific capabilities. Experiments demonstrate that LONG3R outperforms state-of-the-art streaming methods, particularly for longer sequences, while maintaining real-time inference speed. Project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Dynamically filtering irrelevant memory tokens significantly reduces computational load and boosts inference speed (20%) while maintaining reconstruction accuracy.
An interleaved dual-source decoder architecture effectively leverages both immediate temporal context and long-term memory for superior scene reconstruction by progressively aligning features.

Adaptive 3D spatio-temporal memory with pruning is crucial for mitigating cumulative drift and maintaining geometric consistency over extended sequences, proving more effective than fixed memory frameworks.
originalProblem: Existing learning-based streaming 3D reconstruction methods suffer from inefficient memory utilization and spatial redundancy, limiting their effectiveness for long sequences.
Prior methods accumulate geometric drift and struggle with error propagation over extended temporal observations without robust global alignment mechanisms.
Current training strategies are suboptimal for preparing models to handle the complexities and scale of long-sequence data, hindering generalization capabilities.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction accuracy on 7Scenes and NRGBD datasets among online streaming methods while operating in real-time (approx. 22 FPS), and robustly suppressed cumulative drift on long sequences like Replica-100/200.
Outperformed previous streaming methods in camera pose estimation on static scenes (7Scenes, ScanNet) and remained competitive on dynamic scenes (TUM Dynamics).

Ablation studies confirmed that memory gating reduced memory tokens by 27% (on 7Scenes) and increased inference speed by 20%, demonstrating the necessity of the dynamic 3D spatio-temporal memory for long-sequence geometric consistency.
solution: Introduces an attention-based memory gating mechanism and a dynamic 3D spatio-temporal memory with adaptive voxel sizing and pruning to efficiently manage and filter relevant historical information.
Employs a Dual-Source Refined Decoder to integrate filtered memory with immediate temporal context for robust 3D pointmap reconstruction.
Utilizes a two-stage curriculum training strategy for progressively longer sequences, optimizing the model for long-range dependencies.
paper_summary.summary: LONG3R, a recurrent neural network, performs real-time streaming 3D reconstruction over long image sequences, leveraging dynamic memory management and a two-stage curriculum training strategy. Developed by researchers from Shanghai AI Lab, Tsinghua University, and Shanghai Qi Zhi Institute, it achieves consistent accuracy and near-constant memory usage at approximately 22 FPS, mitigating cumulative drift.

title: LONG3R: Long Sequence Streaming 3D Reconstruction

abstract: Online novel view synthesis remains challenging, requiring robust scene reconstruction from sequential, often unposed, observations. We present ReCoSplat, an autoregressive feed-forward Gaussian Splatting model supporting posed or unposed inputs, with or without camera intrinsics. While assembling local Gaussians using camera poses scales better than canonical-space prediction, it creates a dilemma during training: using ground-truth poses ensures stability but causes a distribution mismatch when predicted poses are used at inference. To address this, we introduce a Render-and-Compare (ReCo) module. ReCo renders the current reconstruction from the predicted viewpoint and compares it with the incoming observation, providing a stable conditioning signal that compensates for pose errors. To support long sequences, we propose a hybrid KV cache compression strategy combining early-layer truncation with chunk-level selective retention, reducing the KV cache size by over 90% for 100+ frames. ReCoSplat achieves state-of-the-art performance across different input settings on both in- and out-of-distribution benchmarks. Code and pretrained models will be released. Our project page is at this https URL .

keyInsights: A Render-and-Compare mechanism effectively bridges the gap between training with ground-truth poses and inferring with predicted poses by providing a stable geometric and visual prior to Gaussian prediction.

Efficient memory scaling for autoregressive transformers can be achieved by a combined approach of early-layer KV cache truncation and selective chunk-wise context retention, reducing VRAM by over 90% for long sequences with minimal performance impact.
Explicitly marking retained views in the KV cache with a prompt register token enhances performance, particularly with irregular camera trajectories, by improving context utilization.
originalProblem: Real-time online novel view synthesis from sequential image streams is challenging due to the high computational cost of per-scene optimization in existing Gaussian Splatting techniques.
Feed-forward Gaussian Splatting models primarily target offline scenarios, limiting their use in autoregressive settings where observations arrive sequentially.
Autoregressive feed-forward Gaussian Splatting struggles with a pose distribution mismatch during inference and memory scalability for long sequences due to transformer KV-cache limitations.
results: ReCoSplat achieved state-of-the-art performance in novel view synthesis and camera pose estimation compared to existing autoregressive methods across diverse datasets (DL3DV, ScanNet++, RealEstate10K, ACID, ScanNet).

The model demonstrated robustness in unposed settings, producing sharper geometry and fewer artifacts, and was competitive with offline Gaussian Splatting baselines, even matching YoNoSplat's PSNR in fully posed scenarios.

The KV cache compression strategy reduced VRAM usage by over 90% for long sequences, enabling processing of hundreds of frames on consumer-grade hardware with minimal impact on reconstruction quality.
solution: Develops ReCoSplat, an autoregressive feed-forward Gaussian Splatting model that processes sequential image streams in non-overlapping chunks for real-time scene updates.

Introduces the Render-and-Compare (ReCo) module to address the pose distribution mismatch by conditioning Gaussian prediction on a comparison between the current input image and a rendered view of the accumulated scene.
Implements a KV cache compression strategy, combining early layer truncation and selective context retention, to efficiently handle long image sequences and reduce VRAM usage.
paper_summary.summary: ReCoSplat introduces an autoregressive feed-forward Gaussian Splatting method for real-time 3D scene reconstruction and novel view synthesis from sequential image streams. The model incorporates a Render-and-Compare module to address pose distribution mismatch and a KV cache compression strategy for efficient processing of long sequences, achieving superior performance over other online methods and allowing execution on standard hardware.

title: ReCoSplat: Autoregressive Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting Using Render-and-Compare

abstract: Streaming recurrent models enable efficient 3D reconstruction by maintaining persistent state representations. However, they suffer from catastrophic forgetting over long sequences due to balancing historical information with new observations. Recent methods alleviate this by deriving adaptive signals from attention perspective, but they operate on single dimensions without considering temporal and spatial consistency. To this end, we propose a training-free framework termed TTSA3R that leverages both temporal state evolution and spatial observation quality for adaptive state updates in 3D reconstruction. In particular, we devise a Temporal Adaptive Update Module that regulates update magnitude by analyzing temporal state evolution patterns. Then, a Spatial Contextual Update Module is introduced to localize spatial regions that require updates through observation-state alignment and scene dynamics. These complementary signals are finally fused to determine the state updating strategies. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TTSA3R in diverse 3D tasks. Moreover, our method exhibits only 1.33x error increase compared to over 4x degradation in the baseline model on extended sequences of 3D reconstruction, significantly improving long-term reconstruction stability. Our codes are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Catastrophic forgetting in recurrent streaming 3D reconstruction can be effectively mitigated by implementing adaptive, training-free state update mechanisms.
A comprehensive adaptive strategy benefits from explicitly modeling both the temporal evolution of state tokens and the spatial quality of new observations.

Fusing complementary temporal and spatial update signals provides robust, fine-grained control over the persistent state, crucial for balancing historical information preservation and effective integration of new data.
originalProblem: Recurrent streaming 3D reconstruction models suffer from "catastrophic forgetting," where uniform state update strategies indiscriminately overwrite historical scene information.
This leads to accumulated errors, pose drift, and severe geometric distortions, limiting their long-term accuracy and generalization over extended input sequences.

Existing adaptive update mechanisms are often insufficient as they rely on single-dimensional signals, failing to capture the complex interplay of temporal state evolution and spatial observation quality.
results: TTSA3R maintains significantly more stable video depth and camera pose estimation over extended sequences (up to 800 frames) compared to baseline streaming methods like CUT3R and TTT3R.

It achieves competitive performance on standard benchmarks, including the best Abs Rel error and δ < 1.25 metric on the KITTI dataset for video depth, and lowest Absolute Translation Error (ATE) on TUM-dynamics and ScanNet among streaming methods.
The method operates with a low and constant GPU memory footprint (5 GB) and maintains a competitive inference speed of 18.5 FPS, demonstrating efficiency for real-time applications.
solution: TTSA3R introduces a Training-Free Temporal Adaptive Update Module (TAUM) to quantify state token stability and selectively update dynamic regions while preserving stable information.
A Spatial Context Update Module (SCUM) identifies spatially relevant updates by combining image feature divergence with aggregated cross-attention, focusing on areas with actual scene changes.

A final adaptive mask, derived from the fusion of temporal and spatial masks, enables fine-grained, per-token control over the persistent state update during inference, balancing new information with historical context.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of California, Davis developed TTSA3R, a training-free method that enhances streaming 3D reconstruction by introducing a temporal-spatial adaptive update mechanism for recurrent models' persistent state. This approach prevents catastrophic forgetting, showing significantly more stable video depth and camera pose estimation over long sequences compared to baselines, while maintaining competitive speed and low memory footprint.

title: TTSA3R: Training-Free Temporal-Spatial Adaptive Persistent State for Streaming 3D Reconstruction

abstract: Reconstructing dynamic 4D scenes remains challenging due to the presence of moving objects that corrupt camera pose estimation. Existing optimization methods alleviate this issue with additional supervision, but they are mostly computationally expensive and impractical in real-time applications. To address these limitations, we propose MoRe, a feedforward 4D reconstruction network that efficiently recovers dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos. Built upon a strong static reconstruction backbone, MoRe employs an attention-forcing strategy to disentangle dynamic motion from static structure. To further enhance robustness, we fine-tune the model on large-scale, diverse datasets encompassing both dynamic and static scenes. Moreover, our grouped causal attention captures temporal dependencies and adapts to varying token lengths across frames, ensuring temporally coherent geometry reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that MoRe achieves high-quality dynamic reconstructions with exceptional efficiency.

keyInsights: Guiding the camera token's attention away from dynamic regions towards static structures during training (motion-aligned attention) significantly enhances camera pose accuracy in dynamic scenes without requiring explicit motion priors at inference.

A specialized "grouped causal attention" mechanism, which ensures temporal causality between frames but allows full bidirectional attention within each frame, effectively preserves intra-frame spatial coherence while maintaining temporal reasoning in streaming 4D reconstruction.

A post-hoc, global attention pass over cached camera queries, analogous to bundle adjustment, is crucial for refining camera parameters and improving long-term pose consistency in streaming reconstruction pipelines.
originalProblem: Traditional 4D reconstruction methods often fail in dynamic environments due to object deformation, significant movement, or complex camera motion, as they assume static scenes.

Existing deep learning approaches are either computationally expensive for real-time streaming (hybrid optimization pipelines) or lack accuracy and generalization in dynamic scenes (real-time feed-forward models).

Transformer-based solutions suffer from quadratic scaling inference costs with sequence length, struggle with error accumulation in streaming, and typically do not explicitly account for scene motion.
results: MoRe achieved an inference speed of 30.09 Frames Per Second (FPS) on the KITTI dataset, making it one of the fastest evaluated methods while maintaining high reconstruction accuracy.

The framework consistently outperformed existing streaming-based methods on dynamic benchmarks (Sintel, TUM-dynamics, Bonn) in both camera pose and depth estimation, demonstrating superior robustness in dynamic environments.

Ablation studies confirmed that the motion-aligned attention, grouped causal attention, and bundle-adjustment-like refinement mechanisms each contribute significantly to improving pose accuracy, depth estimation, and overall temporal consistency.
solution: MoRe introduces a feed-forward Transformer network for streaming input that jointly predicts per-frame depth maps, camera poses, point maps, and motion masks from monocular video sequences.

A "motion-aligned attention" mechanism guides the camera token's attention towards static regions during training, enabling the model to disentangle dynamic motion from static scene structure without adding inference time overhead.

Employs "grouped causal attention" for efficient streaming, enforcing temporal causality across frames while allowing full attention within tokens of the same frame, complemented by a "bundle-adjustment-like token aggregation" for global consistency refinement.
paper_summary.summary: MoRe, a motion-aware feed-forward Transformer, reconstructs dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos by jointly predicting depth maps, camera poses, and motion masks. This framework achieves real-time inference at 30.09 FPS while robustly maintaining high accuracy in camera pose and depth estimation, outperforming existing streaming methods on dynamic benchmarks by disentangling motion from static structures without inference overhead.

title: MoRe: Motion-aware Feed-forward 4D Reconstruction Transformer

abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting achieves high-fidelity novel view synthesis, but its application to online long-sequence scenarios is still limited. Existing methods either rely on slow per-scene optimization or fail to provide efficient incremental updates, hindering continuous performance. In this paper, we propose LongSplat, an online real-time 3D Gaussian reconstruction framework designed for long-sequence image input. The core idea is a streaming update mechanism that incrementally integrates current-view observations while selectively compressing redundant historical Gaussians. Crucial to this mechanism is our Gaussian-Image Representation (GIR), a representation that encodes 3D Gaussian parameters into a structured, image-like 2D format. GIR simultaneously enables efficient fusion of current-view and historical Gaussians and identity-aware redundancy compression. These functions enable online reconstruction and adapt the model to long sequences without overwhelming memory or computational costs. Furthermore, we leverage an existing image compression method to guide the generation of more compact and higher-quality 3D Gaussians. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that LongSplat achieves state-of-the-art efficiency-quality trade-offs in real-time novel view synthesis, delivering real-time reconstruction while reducing Gaussian counts by 44\% compared to existing per-pixel Gaussian prediction methods.

keyInsights: Fusing current-view features with historical features, derived from previously rendered Gaussians via GIR, significantly enhances the model's ability to maintain temporal consistency and leverage long-range context.

Representing 3D Gaussians in a 2D image-like format (GIR) with unique IDs allows for efficient memory management, localized gradient updates, and facilitates the computation of 3D overlap metrics for intelligent compression.

A learnable masking strategy, supervised by a 3D IoU metric, is crucial for adaptively compressing redundant Gaussians, leading to both reduced Gaussian count and improved visual clarity without compromising fidelity.
originalProblem: Traditional 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) requires lengthy per-scene optimization, making it impractical for real-time online applications in dynamic environments.

Generalizable 3DGS methods suffer from accumulating redundant and noisy Gaussians, leading to escalating memory/computational costs and quality degradation when processing long-sequence or dense multi-view inputs.
Existing generalizable and sequential 3DGS approaches lack effective mechanisms for global historical context modeling and efficient incremental updates, limiting scalability and consistency.
results: LongSplat achieved superior performance on the DL3DV benchmark, outperforming DepthSplat by up to 2.32 dB PSNR (23.71 vs. 21.39) on dense (50 views) configurations and showing robustness with 120 views.
The method successfully reduced Gaussian counts by over 44% at 50 and 120 views compared to existing per-pixel prediction methods, significantly mitigating memory and computational overhead.
Qualitative comparisons demonstrated that LongSplat produced sharper and more consistent reconstructions with clearer geometry and fewer visual artifacts compared to baselines, even with compression.
solution: A streaming update mechanism incrementally integrates current-view observations, efficiently updating a global 3D Gaussian scene representation by fusing current and historical features.

The Gaussian-Image Representation (GIR) projects 3D Gaussians into a structured 2D format, facilitating efficient history fusion, localized updates, identity-aware compression, and 2D-based supervision.

An adaptive compression module, guided by a learned mask and a view-asymmetric 3D Intersection-over-Union (IoU) metric, selectively prunes redundant historical Gaussians to maintain compactness and quality.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Beijing Institute of Technology, Shenzhen MSU-BIT University, and others developed LongSplat, an online generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting framework. This system enables real-time 3D reconstruction from long-sequence images, achieving over 44% reduction in Gaussian counts compared to prior generalizable methods while improving PSNR by up to 2.32 dB on dense benchmarks.

title: LongSplat: Online Generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting from Long Sequence Images

abstract: Long-sequence streaming 3D reconstruction remains a significant open challenge. Existing autoregressive models often fail when processing long sequences because they anchor poses to the first frame, leading to attention decay, scale drift, and extrapolation errors. We introduce LongStream, a novel gauge-decoupled streaming visual geometry model for metric-scale scene reconstruction across thousands of frames under a strictly online, future-invisible setting. Our approach is threefold. First, we discard the first-frame anchor and predict keyframe-relative poses. This reformulates long-range extrapolation into a constant-difficulty local task. Second, we introduce orthogonal scale learning. This method fully disentangles geometry from scale estimation to suppress drift. Finally, we identify attention bias issues in Transformers, including attention-sink reliance and long-term KV-cache saturation. We propose cache-consistent training combined with periodic cache refresh. This approach suppresses attention biases and contamination over ultra-long sequences and reduces the gap between training and inference. Experiments show that LongStream achieves state-of-the-art performance, enabling stable, metric-scale reconstruction over kilometer-scale sequences at 18 FPS. Project Page: this https URL

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Decoupling global coordinate (SE(3)) and metric scale (Sim(3)) invariances is crucial for stable and robust streaming 3D geometry over long sequences.
Regressing poses relative to local keyframes, rather than an absolute first frame, transforms the challenging long-range extrapolation problem into a manageable local estimation task.
Addressing Transformer attention biases through cache-consistent training and periodic cache refresh is essential for preventing long-term degradation and improving temporal stability.
originalProblem: Existing 3D reconstruction methods are mostly offline, leading to computational redundancy and precluding real-time use for long video streams.
Prior streaming Transformer models for visual geometry experience catastrophic extrapolation failure and scale drift on long sequences, collapsing within tens of meters.
These models struggle due to a 'gauge-coupled' design, anchoring poses to the first frame, and issues like attention sink reliance and KV-cache saturation.
results: LongStream consistently recorded the lowest Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) across diverse indoor and outdoor datasets, achieving an ATE of 51.90 on KITTI compared to 226.15 for the next best streaming baseline.

The model produced stable, coherent poses and maintained accurate metric scale (0.9905 scale ratio on vKITTI) across kilometer-level sequences, including successful loop closure without explicit modules.
LongStream operates in real-time at 18 FPS on a single GPU, exhibiting stable memory usage and latency, avoiding out-of-memory issues faced by other methods on long sequences.
solution: A gauge-decoupled formulation is introduced, predicting poses relative to preceding keyframes to achieve SE(3) invariance and transform long-range extrapolation into a local task.
Orthogonal scale learning disentangles geometry from metric scale estimation, providing Sim(3) invariance and reducing scale drift.
Cache-consistent training (CCT) and periodic cache refresh mitigate attention-sink reliance and KV-cache saturation, improving temporal stability and reducing train-inference mismatch.
paper_summary.summary: LongStream introduces a streaming autoregressive visual geometry model that achieves stable, metric-scale 3D reconstruction for kilometer-scale sequences in real time. It accomplishes this by employing a gauge-decoupled formulation for pose and scale, alongside cache-consistent training and periodic cache refresh to mitigate Transformer attention biases.

title: LongStream: Long-Sequence Streaming Autoregressive Visual Geometry

abstract:
Recent advancements in dynamic 3D scene reconstruction have shown promising
results, enabling high-fidelity 3D novel view synthesis with improved temporal
consistency. Among these, 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) has emerged as an
appealing approach due to its ability to model high-fidelity spatial and
temporal variations. However, existing methods suffer from substantial
computational and memory overhead due to the redundant allocation of 4D
Gaussians to static regions, which can also degrade image quality. In this
work, we introduce hybrid 3D-4D Gaussian Splatting (3D-4DGS), a novel framework
that adaptively represents static regions with 3D Gaussians while reserving 4D
Gaussians for dynamic elements. Our method begins with a fully 4D Gaussian
representation and iteratively converts temporally invariant Gaussians into 3D,
significantly reducing the number of parameters and improving computational
efficiency. Meanwhile, dynamic Gaussians retain their full 4D representation,
capturing complex motions with high fidelity. Our approach achieves
significantly faster training times compared to baseline 4D Gaussian Splatting
methods while maintaining or improving the visual quality.
title: Hybrid 3D-4D Gaussian Splatting for Fast Dynamic Scene Representation

abstract: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes from monocular input is fundamentally under-constrained, with ambiguities arising from occlusion and extreme novel views. While dynamic Gaussian Splatting offers an efficient representation, vanilla models optimize all Gaussian primitives uniformly, ignoring whether they are well or poorly observed. This limitation leads to motion drifts under occlusion and degraded synthesis when extrapolating to unseen views. We argue that uncertainty matters: Gaussians with recurring observations across views and time act as reliable anchors to guide motion, whereas those with limited visibility are treated as less reliable. To this end, we introduce USplat4D, a novel Uncertainty-aware dynamic Gaussian Splatting framework that propagates reliable motion cues to enhance 4D reconstruction. Our approach estimates time-varying per-Gaussian uncertainty and leverages it to construct a spatio-temporal graph for uncertainty-aware optimization. Experiments on diverse real and synthetic datasets show that explicitly modeling uncertainty consistently improves dynamic Gaussian Splatting models, yielding more stable geometry under occlusion and high-quality synthesis at extreme viewpoints.
title: Uncertainty Matters in Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Monocular 4D Reconstruction

abstract: Online 3D reconstruction from streaming inputs requires both long-term temporal consistency and efficient memory usage. Although causal variants of VGGT address this challenge through a key-value (KV) cache mechanism, the cache grows linearly with the stream length, creating a major memory bottleneck. Under limited memory budgets, early cache eviction significantly degrades reconstruction quality and temporal consistency. In this work, we observe that attention in causal transformers for 3D reconstruction exhibits intrinsic spatio-temporal sparsity. Based on this insight, we propose STAC, a Spatio-Temporally Aware Cache Compression framework for streaming 3D reconstruction with large causal transformers. STAC consists of three key components: (1) a Working Temporal Token Caching mechanism that preserves long-term informative tokens using decayed cumulative attention scores; (2) a Long-term Spatial Token Caching scheme that compresses spatially redundant tokens into voxel-aligned representations for memory-efficient storage; and (3) a Chunk-based Multi-frame Optimization strategy that jointly processes consecutive frames to improve temporal coherence and GPU efficiency. Extensive experiments show that STAC achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality while reducing memory consumption by nearly 10x and accelerating inference by 4x, substantially improving the scalability of real-time 3D reconstruction in streaming settings.
title: STAC: Plug-and-Play Spatio-Temporal Aware Cache Compression for Streaming 3D Reconstruction

abstract: Realistic reconstruction of dynamic 4D scenes from monocular videos is essential for understanding the physical world. Despite recent progress in neural rendering, existing methods often struggle to recover accurate 3D geometry and temporally consistent motion in complex environments.
